text.title: IP-Sensitive Workflow Design Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every IP-Sensitive Workflow Design application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Personal-project work MUST happen on a personally-owned device. Mixing on the day-job laptop is rejected even briefly.
rule.rationale: Device ownership is the cleanest provenance signal an arbitrator will accept; one mixed commit on company gear can shift the IP attribution argument.

rule.statement: Personal-project credentials (GitHub, registry, deploy keys) MUST live in a credential store distinct from any day-job vault. Shared 1Password vaults are rejected.
rule.rationale: Credential co-mingling indicates resource-mixing; separating vaults preserves the 'not on company resources' defence.

rule.statement: Commit timestamps MUST fall outside contracted day-job working hours OR be explicitly marked PTO/unpaid in the day-job timesheet. Working-hour commits are flagged.
rule.rationale: Time-of-commit is the second-strongest evidence after device; the fence prevents 'on company time' disputes.

rule.statement: Personal repos MUST live under a personal GitHub/GitLab namespace, not a shared org. Cross-org forks are rejected.
rule.rationale: Org membership is sticky; even an apparently personal fork inside the client's org can be claimed.

rule.statement: Every commit MUST emit an IP-attribution log entry: device id, location, hours-fenced flag, credential store id. Logs MUST be retained 7 years.
rule.rationale: When a dispute arises, the log is the contemporaneous record; without it the defence relies on memory and metadata, both weak.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
